claim: Ground-level PM2.5 monitoring in South Asia is dangerously sparse: India has approximately 300 continuous monitoring stations for 1.4 billion people (roughly 1 per 4.7 million), with Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Nepal having even fewer stations accessible through public platforms. Where monitors are absent, satellite-derived estimates and column observations provide the only exposure data, but these carry large uncertainty in South Asia due to complex aerosol composition and monsoon cloud cover.
failure_modes: A reader treats satellite-derived PM2.5 estimates as equivalent to ground monitor measurements in unmonitored rural areas, where validation data does not exist.
A reader treats the 300-station figure as static; station counts change with new installations, outages, and reporting changes.
A reader assumes OpenAQ coverage reflects actual monitoring density, when the platform aggregates available feeds and may miss stations that do not publish open data.
A reader treats TROPOMI column NO2 observations as a direct PM2.5 measurement, when the column-to-surface conversion adds modeled uncertainty on top of the NO2-to-PM2.5 proxy assumption.
A reader uses population-weighted PM2.5 exposure from HEI State of Global Air to infer district-level exposure, when the 0.01-degree resolution may smooth over localized hotspots.
Low-cost sensor networks are deployed in South Asia without prior co-located validation against reference monitors in high-humidity, high-temperature conditions.
kill_condition: A reviewer identifies a public dataset showing that South Asian ground monitoring density exceeds 1 station per 1 million population, or finds a peer-reviewed validation study demonstrating that satellite-derived PM2.5 estimates in South Asian rural areas have RMSE below 10 ug/m3 against co-located reference monitors.
limitations: The 300-station figure for India is from CPCB as of 2023 and may be outdated.
Station counts for Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Nepal are inferred from OpenAQ coverage, not from official national pollution control agency data.
The claim about satellite uncertainty in South Asia is supported by methodology documentation and known aerosol composition complexity, not by a specific South Asian validation study.
Low-cost sensor rejection is based on the absence of validation data, not on evidence that sensors are inaccurate in South Asia.
